# COMP110 Survey Analysis

## Summary
This project analyzes student survey responses to explore the relationship between perceived difficulty and understanding. The goal is to determine whether students who find the course more difficult also report higher levels of understanding.

## Visualizations

### Difficulty vs Understanding
<img src="/myproject/static/imgs/graph1.png" width="500"/>

### Distribution of Difficulty
<img src="/myproject/static/imgs/graph2.png" width="500"/>

### Distribution of Understanding
<img src="/myproject/static/imgs/graph3.png" width="500"/>

## Conclusion
Based on the analysis, many students perceive the course as moderately to highly difficult, with a large number of responses at difficulty levels 4 and above. However, the distribution of understanding remains centered around mid to higher values, suggesting that students are generally still able to grasp the material despite the perceived challenge. Additionally, the relationship between difficulty and understanding does not show a strong negative trend, indicating that increased difficulty does not necessarily lead to lower comprehension.

These findings suggest that while the course may feel challenging, it is not overwhelmingly difficult for most students and may be appropriately calibrated to promote learning. Therefore, rather than significantly reducing difficulty, a more effective improvement could be to better support students during more challenging topics, such as providing clearer explanations, additional examples, or more structured guidance.

One possible extension of this analysis would be to examine difficulty and understanding at the topic level, since the current dataset does not distinguish between different course concepts. Collecting more granular data could help identify specific areas where students struggle most.

A potential trade-off of reducing difficulty too much is that it may lower the rigor of the course and reduce learning outcomes for students who benefit from being challenged. Additionally, it may set up students worse for future computer science classes especially if they are interested in pursuing the degree. Instead, targeted improvements would better balance challenge and comprehension while maintaining course quality.
